title: The Accounting Cycle & Journal Entries
subhead: The equation that governs every entry ever made, the chart of accounts that organizes them, and the cycle that turns raw transactions into auditable financial statements.
hook: "Post this journal entry." A real controller does not hand you a pre-filled form. They hand you a vendor invoice and expect you to know which accounts move, in which direction, and why. This module is the entire reason that expectation exists.

chunks.title: The accounting equation and the T-account
chunks.bodyHtml:
Assets = Liabilities + Equity is not a formula to memorize for a test. It is a constraint that governs every single entry ever posted in every company on earth. The day you make a journal entry that violates it, your books are wrong — and a trial balance will tell you only minutes later.

Every account lives in a ledger, and the visual representation of a single account's activity is a T-account: debits on the left, credits on the right. Which side increases the balance depends on the account's type:

Account typeNormal balanceIncrease with
AssetsDebitDebit (left)
ExpensesDebitDebit (left)
LiabilitiesCreditCredit (right)
EquityCreditCredit (right)
RevenueCreditCredit (right)

Skarion's five largest asset accounts on the balance sheet are: 1000 Operating Checking, 1100 Accounts Receivable, 1400 Prepaid Software Licenses (ArcGIS Pro, Vetro FiberMap), 1500 GIS Servers & Workstations, and the Security Deposit on office space held by the landlord. Every one of those accounts increases with a debit.

callout.html: Stop thinking "debit = good." Debits are directional, not evaluative. A debit to Cash is good. A debit to Accounts Receivable means a customer owes you more (also fine). A debit to Salary Expense means you owe your staff more money (less fine). The side tells you the direction of movement, not whether the business is winning.

chunks.title: Chart of Accounts deep dive
chunks.bodyHtml:
A Chart of Accounts (COA) is the numbered list of every account the company uses. The numbering is convention, not fashion: 1000s are Assets, 2000s are Liabilities, 3000s are Equity, 4000s are Revenue, and 5000–6999 are Expenses (including COGS). Every company's COA is slightly different — learn yours on day one, then never invent accounts on your own.

Skarion Engineering's actual COA (the one you will use all course):

CodeAccountType
1000Operating CheckingAsset
1100Accounts ReceivableAsset
1200Unbilled AR (WIP)Asset
1400Prepaid Software LicensesAsset
1500GIS Servers & WorkstationsAsset
1590Accumulated Depreciationcontra-Asset
2000Accounts PayableLiability
2100Accrued WagesLiability
2200Payroll Tax LiabilitiesLiability
2300Accrued InterestLiability
2500Deferred RevenueLiability
3000Owner's EquityEquity
3100Retained EarningsEquity
4000Engineering Design RevenueRevenue
4100Permitting Services RevenueRevenue
5100COGS — Subcontractor DraftingExpense
5200COGS — Software Allocated to ProjectsExpense
6000Salaries — US Engineering PMsExpense
6100Payroll Tax ExpenseExpense
6400Travel & Field ExpenseExpense
6500Software & SubscriptionsExpense
6700Depreciation ExpenseExpense
6800Interest ExpenseExpense

Memorize the structure: that block of five-digit numbers in the database is not chaos, it is a balance sheet and an income statement waiting to be rolled up.

chunks.title: Classify the transaction into the correct account
chunks.instruction: Drag each Skarion Engineering transaction into the account bucket it should be posted to. Getting this right is the single skill that separates a real staff accountant from someone who memorized the textbook.

items.draggable: Wire from Atlantic Fiber pays an open invoice
Prepaid Vetro license amortized for the month
Global CAD Solutions invoice for offshore drafting
Client prepays a contract before work starts
HLD milestone delivered to Tennessee Corridor
Offshore drafting billed on a project
Employer FICA on the US payroll run
Interest on the term loan for January

chunks.title: Journal entries — the full workflow
chunks.bodyHtml:
A journal entry is the atomic unit of accounting. Everything else — the trial balance, the financial statements, the audit — is built on top of them. Every journal entry must contain:

  • Date — the period the entry belongs to (cutoff matters).
  • Debit account(s) and amounts.
  • Credit account(s) and amounts.
  • Memo — what the entry is for, in plain language.
  • Supporting document reference — invoice number, PO number, wire confirmation, timesheet batch. Nobody posts an entry without documentation.

The golden rule, with no exceptions: debits = credits on every entry, always. A compound entry uses multiple debits or multiple credits in a single entry (payroll is the classic example — one cash credit, several liability credits, an expense debit, and a tax expense debit). Compound entries are normal and encouraged; just keep the two sides equal.

callout.html: What a memo actually sounds like: "Record Jan offshore drafting per GCS-2026-091, 28-day terms, Tennessee Corridor build." That memo tells any auditor exactly what happened, why, and where the source document lives. A memo that says "AP" is a firing offense at most firms.

chunks.title: Record an offshore drafting invoice
chunks.scenarioHtml: Global CAD Solutions invoices Skarion Engineering for $7,200 of LLD (low-level design) drafting on 160 miles of fiber route. No cash has moved yet — the bill has 30-day terms. Record the liability on receipt of the invoice.

chunks.title: Capitalize an annual software license
chunks.scenarioHtml: Skarion pays $36,000 up front for a 12-month Vetro FiberMap enterprise license, wired from the operating checking account. Because the benefit covers the whole year, you capitalize it as a Prepaid Asset — not expense it all at once.

chunks.title: Record an engineer payroll run
chunks.scenarioHtml: The US engineering payroll run totals $45,000 in gross wages. Of that, $9,500 is withheld for employee taxes and remitted to the government later. The remaining $35,500 net pay is wired out of Operating Checking to the employees. This is a compound entry — three lines, two credits, one debit, balanced.

chunks.title: The accounting cycle, end to end

steps.title: 1. Source document
steps.desc: Invoice, timesheet, wire confirmation, PO. Every entry starts with proof — never an assumption.

steps.title: 2. Journal entry
steps.desc: Analyze the document, identify the accounts, and record the debit(s) and credit(s) in the journal. Debits must equal credits.

steps.title: 3. Post to the GL
steps.desc: The journal entry flows into each account in the general ledger. This is what builds the running balance of every account over time.

steps.title: 4. Trial balance
steps.desc: List every account and its balance. Total debits should equal total credits — if not, you made a posting error and you stop here until you find it.

steps.title: 5. Adjusting entries
steps.desc: Accruals, prepaids, depreciation, WIP revenue. These entries make the books match economic reality before the statements are produced.

steps.title: 6. Adjusted trial balance
steps.desc: Re-run the trial balance after adjustments. Still must balance — and now it reflects accrual-basis reality.

steps.title: 7. Financial statements
steps.desc: The adjusted trial balance rolls up into the Income Statement, Balance Sheet, and Cash Flow Statement. This is the deliverable management reads.

steps.title: 8. Close temporary accounts
steps.desc: Revenue and expense accounts are closed to Retained Earnings so the new period starts clean. Asset, Liability, and Equity accounts carry forward.

chunks.intro: Four questions on the cycle you just walked. These come up in interviews — verbatim.
chunks.title: Accounting cycle & trial balance quiz

questions.q: Skarion posts a $7,200 offshore drafting bill (no cash has moved). Which account is debited?
questions.explanation: The cost of subcontractor drafting is an expense (COGS) that increases with a debit. The liability to the vendor (AP) is credited. Cash is untouched until you pay the bill.

questions.q: Skarion records a $36,000 prepaid Vetro license by debiting Prepaid Software and crediting Cash. Which account does NOT change in that entry?
questions.explanation: Only Prepaid Software (debit) and Cash (credit) move. Software Expense does not change at purchase — the $3,000/month expense comes later through amortization adjusting entries.

questions.q: You run the trial balance and total debits equal total credits. What does that actually prove?
options: Every entry was correct and posted to the right account
The financial statements are ready to publish
Every entry had equal debits and credits — nothing more
There are no timing differences left to adjust
questions.explanation: A balanced trial balance proves only that debits equal credits mechanically. It says nothing about whether the right accounts were used or whether accruals are correct. It is a necessary check, not proof of accuracy.

questions.q: At year-end, which accounts are closed to Retained Earnings?
options: All accounts are closed at year-end
Only the Cash account is closed
questions.explanation: Temporary accounts — Revenue and Expense — are closed to Retained Earnings so the next period starts at zero. Permanent accounts (Asset, Liability, Equity) carry their balances forward to the next period.
